However, since the cost to enter is only 100-300 chips and I am deep-stacked, I will open with a standard raise to 300 to define the hand. This allows me to set the price and potentially see a flop where I can set-mine.

KTo offsuit on the button facing an UTG raise from Flash to 300 (3x). Flash has been aggressive in previous hands and plays from early position suggest a strong range (top 10-15%: pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc.). My hand has poor playability postflop (offsuit, no suited connectors), and equity against that range is around 35%, but with two players to act behind (SB and BB who defend wide sometimes), calling risks multiway pots where KTo performs poorly. Position helps, but folding preserves my 1610 stack for better spots. Shoving is too aggressive (3.5x pot raise), raising light risks cooler. Pot odds 4:1 not enough given reverse implied odds. Standard fold.

Holding A♠K♥ (AK offsuit) in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300 — this is a standard 3-bet spot. AK is a premium hand that wants to get value and avoid playing multiway out of position. Pot is 450 and it costs 250 to call; calling keeps us OOP vs the big blind and the original raiser. I want to 3-bet to deny equity to speculative hands, isolate the opener, and build the pot with a top-tier hand. A sizing of ~3x the open (~1000 total) is appropriate here — large enough to apply pressure and charge draws/small pairs but not so large that we overcommit our ~35BB stack. If Flash or Claude 4-bets, I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (call smaller 4-bets, fold to very large ones), but for now raise for value and fold equity.

Folding JQ out of position in a 3-way re-raised pot. While JQ is a decent hand, I'm facing a strong re-raise from Minni (likely pairs or broadway), I'm out of position, and have to navigate unclear post-flop dynamics against two opponents. With a healthy stack, I should conserve chips for stronger positions and situations where I'm not playing from the worst seat at the table. This is a disciplined fold.

I opened from UTG with pocket 3s and Minni 3-bet me to 1000 from the small blind. The pot is 1400 and it costs me 700 to call. Minni's remaining stack is only 1550. For set-mining to be profitable, I typically look for implied odds of at least 10:1 or 15:1. Here, the effective stack remaining is only 1550, which is barely twice the amount I need to call. This provides zero implied odds if I hit my set. Given that pocket 3s have very little showdown value against a 3-betting range and I cannot profitably set-mine due to the shallow effective stacks, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

16BB effective stack UTG in 4-handed play. K5s suited is a marginal hand from early position. While short-handed ranges widen, UTG still requires stronger holdings to voluntarily put money in preflop. Limping is poor with short stack as it leads to multiway pots out of position; small raise commits too much of stack (~20%) with a hand that plays poorly postflop often. Shoving is overplay for UTG spot against calling ranges from button (Minni, shown aggressive) and blinds. Opponents have deeper stacks and history of frequent raises, increasing fold equity but also call risk with better kings/AQ+. Best to fold and preserve stack for premium shoving spots like pairs, Axs, KQs+, suited connectors in better position.

I’m on the button with Tc2c and 39.5bb effective. This is a weak but suited hand —worth playing in position as a steal attempt versus only the blinds. A standard 3x open (to 300) gives me good fold equity and isolates the pot; limp/call invites multiway pots and reduces my post‑flop advantage. Plan: open to 300, fold to a substantial 3‑bet, and if called use position to c‑bet favorable textures and leverage my backdoor club equity.

As2d is the worst hand in poker (worst kicker with an ace). I'm in the small blind facing a button raise from Minni, who has shown aggressive tendencies throughout the session. Several critical factors make folding the correct play: (1) I'm out of position with one of the worst possible holdings, (2) the Big Blind (Flash) hasn't acted yet and could re-raise, further compromising my position, (3) even against Minni's button range, As2o is a significant underdog without sufficient implied odds, and (4) my stack is reasonable enough to avoid desperation plays. This is a clear fold—there's no scenario where playing As2d out of position is profitable long-term.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Sevens. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a stack of 27,900, I have a massive chip lead. Minni has a relatively short stack of 3,650 (36.5 BB). Pocket Sevens is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a button open. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to see a flop and set-mine or play post-flop without committing a large portion of the pot immediately against a short stack that might shove. The odds to call are 200 to win a pot that will be 750, which is very favorable.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d7h. The flop is 9h-8c-8h. Minni, who raised pre-flop from the Button, has a stack of 3,650. This board is relatively neutral but hits a lot of a Button's range (9x, 8x, draws). I have a mid-pocket pair. I am first to act on the flop. Given I am the chip leader and Minni is playing a somewhat shorter stack, checking is the standard play here to see how Minni reacts. My pair of sevens is currently an underpair to the nines but is still ahead of many overcards. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing, as many of her semi-bluffs (like hearts or straight draws) have significant equity against me.

I opened preflop and am the preflop aggressor on the button with Tc2c. Flop 9h 8c 8h: I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor club draw, but the board is fairly dry and the BB checked, showing some weakness. Versus one opponent this is a good spot for a small continuation bet to fold out unpaired overcards and collect the pot now. Pot is 650, so I’ll bet roughly one‑third to preserve stack and keep fold equity (~220). If he calls I’ll proceed very cautiously on the turn — give up to significant raises unless I pick up a pair or meaningful club/runner draw; if he check‑raises I will fold most of my range with this hand.

I have a mid-pocket pair of Sevens on a paired board of 9h-8c-8h. Minni's bet of 220 into a pot of 650 is a small continuation bet, typical for a pre-flop aggressor on this type of board. My Sevens are currently an underpair to the Nine but beat all of her overcard combinations (like AK, AQ, or KJ). Folding to such a small bet would be too exploitable, and raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me. Calling allows me to see a turn card and evaluate further based on her sizing and the board development, while keeping the pot manageable against a shorter-stacked opponent.

On the turn board of 9h-8c-8h-2h, I have a pocket pair of Sevens and a Seven-high flush draw. The 2h completes the heart flush. I checked the flop and called a small bet. Checking again on the turn is the most consistent and safe play. It allows me to see how Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) reacts to the flush-completing card. If she checks behind, I likely have the best hand against her overcards. If she bets, I can re-evaluate based on her sizing, keeping in mind I have a flush draw to improve my hand on the river.

Check. The turn (2h) gives me a very weak second pair (2s) but the board is now paired eights with three hearts (9h 8c 8h 2h). Flash called preflop and my small flop c-bet, so his range contains many hands that currently crush me (two-heart combos that already have a flush, 9x, 8x/trips, overpairs), and betting here will get value from very few worse hands while leaving me vulnerable to a check-raise or being drawn out on the river. My preflop/flop plan was to be cautious on the turn unless I improved to a meaningful hand — this improvement is marginal and not worth building the pot out of position. I’ll control the pot by checking and reevaluate on the river (calling small bets to see a cheap river, folding to large aggression).

On a board of T-9-8-8-2, my pocket Sevens have very little showdown value. My best five-card hand is 8-8-T-9-7. Almost any two overcards from Minni's button-opening range (like AK, QJ, or even A5) will beat me by playing the board with a higher kicker. Minni checked back the turn after the flush draw completed, which could indicate a weak made hand or a complete miss. Since I likely lose to most of her range and have no reason to turn my hand into a bluff against a short stack, I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown if she checks behind with air lower than a Seven.

I improved to a made hand on the river — top pair (Tc + Td). Flash checked the river after calling my flop c-bet and checking the turn, which indicates weakness or a marginal one-pair hand (missed overcards, 9x, or air) rather than a strong, value-heavy line. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) should get called by worse single-pair hands and some curious overcards while keeping the size low enough to avoid bloating the pot against trips/full houses or polarizing check-raises. If Flash check-raises big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll expect to win at showdown most of the time. Bet 360.

Looking at the board of 9h-8c-8h-2h-Td, my best five-card hand is 8-8-T-9-7 (a pair of eights with a ten-nine-seven kicker). Because there are four cards on the board (T, 9, 8, 8) that are all higher than my pocket sevens, my sevens are effectively useless as a 'pair' in the traditional sense; I am playing the board with a seven kicker. Almost any hand in Minni's button-opening range, even her missed hands like A-K, A-J, or K-Q, will beat me because they will play the board with a higher kicker (e.g., A-K plays 8-8-T-9-A). Since I cannot even beat a total bluff from her high-card range, calling is not an option. Minni's 1/3 pot bet is likely a thin value bet or a cheap bluff that still beats my hand.

Short‑handed (4 players) UTG I widen my opening range; Q8d is a playable, suited Qx with good post‑flop playability and a useful queen blocker. I want initiative and to avoid limp‑multiway pots out of position, so I’ll open rather than just call 100. Standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) is appropriate here — larger than the min‑raise to discourage too many callers and isolate the blinds. I note Elon (BB) is short (~15bb) and could shove; I’ll be prepared to fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet from Flash/Claude but can call a short‑stack shove depending on his perceived range and pot odds. Action: open to 300.

Folding A2o (ace-deuce offsuit) from the button. While I have excellent position, A2o is the worst hand in poker and doesn't warrant playing against a UTG raise from Minni. The hand has very limited equity even against a wide range, particularly from an early position raiser. My stack is healthy at 6390 chips, so there's no urgency to get involved with marginal hands. I should preserve chips for premium situations and let this weak hand go.

In Hand #35, I am in the Small Blind holding 6c 7d. Minni, who has been quite active and aggressive throughout the previous hands (e.g., Hand #2, #6, #10, #30, #34), has raised to 300 from UTG. While 6-7 offsuit can be a speculative connector in some positions, playing it out of position against a 3x open from an aggressive UTG player is generally a losing play in the long run. My stack is very healthy (over 270 big blinds), but there is no need to defend with such a weak hand out of position. I will fold.

I have bottom pair (nines) on a very dangerous monotone board (Ah Th 9h). I do not have a heart in my hand. I completely missed this flop. With 4♦6♦ I have no pair, no hearts and only a remote backdoor diamond possibility (needs two running diamonds). Board A♥ T♥ 9♥ is very wet for heart/AX/TX/9X ranges and can easily be in opponents' checking ranges for pot control or trap. On the turn, I have a pair of 9s (9s in hand with 9h on flop). This allows me to: (1) see the river cheaply, (2) keep the pot small given my short stack, (3) avoid committing chips to a hand where I may be behind to draws or higher pairs, and (4) control risk while remaining in the hand. If the river brings a K, Q, or J, I gain additional outs to two pair or a higher pair to improve. Checking preserves my ability to make a better decision on the river with complete information.

On the turn, the board has paired the Ten (Ts) on top of the three hearts already present (Ah Th 9h). My hand is 9c 4h, giving me bottom pair. I hold 4♦6♦ and completely missed this board (A♥ T♥ 9♥ T♠). I have no pair, no heart draw, and a diamond flush is impossible now (would have needed both turn and river). The pot is small (300) and there's no reason to bloat it out of position with a junk hand. Plan: check behind, preserve stack, and fold to any meaningful bet on the river unless I pair or the river gives a very clear bluff opportunity.

On the river with a pair of 9s (board shows Ah Th 9h Ts 4s), I'm in a critical spot with a short stack of 2470 chips. Betting here is risky: (1) Flash's massive 33k stack means he can apply pressure if he has any hand, (2) even Minni at 2870 could call with marginal hands, and (3) with so few chips remaining, I cannot afford to be called and lose. The safe play is to check and take the showdown, where my pair of 9s will likely win or split the pot. This preserves my stack and maximizes my chip preservation for future opportunities where I can get chips in with better equity. The river brought no improvement (4s doesn't help), so checking down is the correct risk management decision.

The board is Ah Th 9h Ts 4s. My hand is 9c 4h, which gives me two pair, Tens and Nines, with an Ace kicker. River completed A♥ T♥ 9♥ T♠ 4♠ and my 4♦ gives me a weak pair of fours. Pot is only 300 and both opponents checked every street, so this is a multiway showdown with low fold equity. Betting for thin value is low EV — most hands that I beat (missed heart draws, single overcards) are unlikely to call a bet after checking the whole way, while hands that beat me (A×, T×, any heart flush, trips/full) either already have me crushed or can easily raise (Flash is very deep/aggressive). I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position or face a raise with a marginal one-pair hand. Best play is to check and take my showdown for free; if faced with a meaningful bet I can fold or evaluate based on sizing.

UTG with Jh8s (offsuit) in a 4-handed cash game is a marginal hand to open. There is a very short-stacked big blind (Elon ~9bb) who is likely to shove over a small raise, and the deep, aggressive small blind (Flash) and capable button (Claude) behind me increase the chance of facing a tough decision post-flop or a shove/3-bet. J8o has poor playability out of position and weak showdown value, so opening or limping would put my ~28.7bb stack into marginal spots. I'll fold and wait for a better spot or a suited/bigger holding in position.

Folding T6o from the button. While position is favorable for stealing blinds, T6o is a weak, unprofitable hand that plays poorly post-flop. The hand lacks both high-card value and drawing potential. Even on the button, I should maintain hand selection discipline and only play hands with reasonable equity. The cost of folding (100 chips) is minimal compared to the negative expected value of playing this weak holding out of position against competent opponents. With 2620 chips and 43 hands played so far, I should preserve chips for premium situations.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 5h, which is an extremely weak hand. The action has folded to me. Elon, in the Big Blind, has a very short stack of 910 chips (about 9 big blinds). JQo from UTG is a solid broadway hand worth playing for value. This is a standard 3x big blind raise (3x100). The hand has good potential to win both pre-flop and post-flop. Flash has a massive 33k stack and will likely fold or make a considered decision. This raise maintains aggression and lets me take down the pot often while building it when called with a good hand. My stack of 2620 allows me to play this hand confidently.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit (As Th). Claude has raised to 300 from UTG (effectively the Cutoff in this 4-handed game). My hand is strong enough to play, especially in position. If Elon (the shortest stack) shoves, I am priced in to call for a small additional amount. A 3-bet is an aggressive play that leverages my chip lead.

Preflop in the small blind with 3h9c, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and no strong blockers. Claude opened UTG to 300 and Flash 3‑bet to 900 from the button. I hold T♦6♥ — a weak, unsuited, poorly coordinated hand that is frequently dominated by both players' 3‑bet and opening ranges. JQo is a solid broadway hand with reasonable equity against Flash's reraise range. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (1350 pot to 600 to call) makes this a mathematically sound call. JQo is a broadway hand but remains unimproved on this dry flop (5d-3s-9h). I'm out of position against Flash who has shown aggression and possesses a dominant stack (32550 vs my 1720). On this very dry board (5d-3s-9h) after 3-betting pre-flop, I maintain a significant range advantage. Claude's check is expected. JQo has completely whiffed on the 5d-3s-9h flop - no pair, no draw, just two overcards with approximately 24% equity at best. I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Ace-Nine offsuit. This hand is well within the opening range for this position. Short stack of ~10BB on the button facing a UTG open from Flash to 300 into 450 pot. QJo offsuit is a playable defending hand in position with good broadway potential and post-flop playability. Facing an UTG open to 300 and a button call from the button, I'm in the small blind with Q♦T♥. 8-10 offsuit is a weak hand that is easily dominated by Flash's raising range from UTG and Elon's call. I have top pair with an Ace and a Nine kicker on a relatively dry board of A-J-3. My opponent, Elon, has a very short stack of only 710 chips remaining while the pot is 750. Short stack of 710 (~7BB) on button with JQo OOP no, IP vs Flash's UTG open and flop c-bet of 300 into 750 (40% pot). Board A- J-3 rainbow favors Flash's strong Ax-heavy range heavily, but we hold second pair (Jd pairs Jh) with live Q kicker, blocking some JJ combos. Folding too tight given position, hand strength blocks sets/overpairs, decent equity vs Ax (20-30% depending on Ax), and superior vs Jx/draws/bluffs in aggro Flash's range (history: frequent big bets, won pots with strong hands like flushes/straights but also check-folds). Raising all-in maximizes fold equity against polarized c-bet range (missed overpairs/draws fold, worse Jx call), extracts value from AJ/KJ/QJ worse kickers/draws, and denies equity realization. Calling risks turn domination; jam denies outs and leverages short stack pressure vs Flash's pot control tendencies in history.

Elon has gone all-in for a total of 710 chips on the flop. I have top pair with an Ace and a Nine kicker on a board of A-J-3. On the button with 8♠2♦ — one of the worst hands. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop (or min-raise to 200). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I'm in the small blind with 9d-Qc. Minni (button) has folded, leaving me heads-up against Flash in the big blind. I need to call 50 more to win a pot of 150, giving me 3:1 pot odds. I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The Small Blind (Claude) has completed the bet. I'm betting my top pair (Queens with 9 kicker) plus a gutshot straight draw on a heads-up flop. With my short stack of 1520 and a solid made hand, I should play actively to build equity. J2o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even in 3-handed play with positional advantage, the hand has poor equity postflop. In the Small Blind with K9o after the Button folds, this is a standard spot to raise and take the initiative against the Big Blind. K9o is well above the average hand range in a heads-up situation. I’m in the Big Blind with A♠5♣ facing a 3x SB open to 300. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (total 300). Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me see a flop with a playable hand. I will call (total commit = 300).

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this relatively dry Queen-high flop. I will lead with a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure and try to take down the pot immediately, as King-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many turn cards.

I have A♠5♣ on 7♦5♥Q♣ — second pair with the Ace kicker. Pot is 900 and Flash bet 300; calling 300 gives ~25% pot odds so I only need ~25% equity. Flash opened wide from the SB and will c-bet many bluffs and overcards, so my hand has good showdown value against a wide range. A raise would be thin and likely to induce a bigger response from Flash's huge stack; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call the 300, evaluate the turn — fold to large aggression unless I improve or get a clear read, and extract value if I hit two-pair or better.

The 3d on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Board: 7♦ 5♥ Q♣ 3♦. I hold A♠5♣ — second pair with the nut kicker. Flash (SB) opened wide and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn. That line typically represents a wide range: pot-control Qx, weaker pairs, floats/missed overcards, and some diamond draws that now have two diamonds on board. With 1,200 in the pot and my stack ~2,120, I don’t want to inflate the pot and face a big raise from Flash’s huge stack with a hand that is vulnerable to Qx and sets. My hand has good showdown value vs his checking range and blocks some strong A♦X hands. Betting thin for value risks getting raised off or committing a large portion of my stack; checking realizes equity and keeps options open on the river. Plan: check and see river; call reasonable river bets for value, fold to clear show of strength (big shove/raise) unless I improve.

On the river Jack of Clubs, the board is Seven, Five, Queen, Trey, Jack. I will check to see if she checks behind with a weaker hand or a missed draw, and I am prepared to fold to a significant bet as my hand is effectively just a bluff-catcher now.

I have A♠5♣ — second pair with the nut kicker on 7♦ 5♥ Q♣ 3♦ J♣. Pot is 1,200 and my stack ~2,120. Flash opened wide preflop, c-bet the flop, then checked turn and river. Given the effective stacks and his very deep stack (he can apply pressure), I don’t want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable second pair. I will check to realize equity and call a reasonable bet; fold to large aggression.

I am currently the chip leader by a significant margin. Holding 9s 7c on the button is a marginal stealing hand. Heads-up from the small blind I should be opening very wide — 9♦7♦ is a playable suited connector with good straight/flush potential and good postflop playability. Raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to apply pressure to Claude’s 16.2BB stack, pick up the blinds often, and isolate him rather than playing a marginal hand OOP by just calling 50. This sizing maximizes immediate fold equity while keeping plenty of chips behind to extract value when appropriate.

I'm in the Big Blind with 2d-4o (deuce-four offsuit), which is the worst hand in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind, and Flash folded, leaving this heads-up. I'm on the button with A♥2♣ in a 3-handed pot (SB ~15.7BB, BB very deep). This is a classic spot to open wider — A2o has good positional value and blocks some Ace combinations. Plan: if Claude (SB) shoves I will strongly consider (often call) given his short-stack shove range and my Ace-blocker; if Flash (BB) 3-bets big I will fold out of position; if called I'll use my position to play postflop and c-bet selectively.

I'm in the small blind with 2d-Qc, one of the worst starting hands in poker. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, and I need to call 250. While the pot odds are decent (450 to call 250), several factors make this a clear fold:

1. Hand Quality: 2-Queen offsuit is essentially unplayable - it has poor win rate against most ranges
2. Position: I'm out of position (small blind) against Minni's raise and Flash still to act in big blind
3. Stack Pressure: My stack is short (1570), so I need to preserve chips and play premium hands
4. Range vs. Position: Minni is correctly aggressive from the button; my out-of-position weakness is magnified with this hand
5. Three-way Risk: There's still Flash to act in the big blind - I could face action from both opponents postflop with a trash hand

I should fold and wait for better opportunities with my remaining chips. Chip preservation is key when short-stacked.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. Minni opened to 300 (3x the big blind) from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), Q6o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against a 3x raise sizing. My opponents are relatively short-stacked compared to me, which reduces my implied odds and increases the risk of being forced into difficult decisions post-flop. I'll fold this hand and wait for more favorable spots.

Premium hand (AK) on the button with excellent position. Raise to 300 (3x BB) to build the pot and establish strength. This sizing is standard for premium holdings and maintains reasonable fold equity while capitalizing on positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 4s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Claude has raised from the Button to 300. Given that my hand has almost no equity and I would be playing out of position against both the Button and the Big Blind, the most optimal and standard play is to fold.

Claude opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with A♣5♥. Versus a 3x button open this is a defendable holding — the Ace is a strong blocker and the hand has enough post‑flop playability to call. Claude’s effective stack is only ~1270 (≈12.7bb), so a min‑3bet to 500 risks getting shoved on and bloating a pot out of position; I don’t want to commit more equity preflop with a marginal offsuit Ace. (If he jams over my call I’ll re‑evaluate vs his shove range — after my call I’d need ≈37% equity to profitably call an all‑in.)

I have A♣5♥ on 2♣-6♣-T♠: only Ace-high plus a very remote (runner-runner) club flush possibility and an important blocker (A♣) to club/ace combos. Claude is on the button with only ~1,270 effective (≈12.7bb) and opens wide frequently, but his short stack means he can shove at any time. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me realize equity, and lets Claude bluff or fold hands that missed. Plan: check and call a small-to-moderate c-bet (to see a turn with top-card/backdoor equity), but fold to a large overbet or shove since my showdown value is weak against his likely continuing range. If he checks back I take the pot now.

Standard continuation bet with premium holding (AK) on a dry flop. Minni's check suggests weakness. Betting 400 (approximately 2/3 pot) maintains aggression, wins many pots outright, and extracts value from weaker hands while I still have multiple ways to improve. Position and pre-flop aggression support this play.

Claude (button) opened wide and his 400 flop c‑bet into a 1050 pot is a relatively small continuation bet. I have A♣5♥ — only Ace‑high plus a very remote runner‑runner club flush, but A♣ is an important blocker to his Ax and club combos. Calling a small bet here keeps the pot manageable, realizes my (thin) showdown equity against a wide BTN range, and prevents getting committed with a marginal hand versus a short‑stack shove. Calling 400 gives me roughly a ~21–22% break‑even equity (400 to win the pot and my call), which is acceptable vs his range. Plan: call to see a turn; fold to significant turn aggression or a shove unless I pair the Ace or pick up a clear draw. I won’t raise — don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal offsuit Ace.

Check. On the Q♠ turn my A♣5♥ has only Ace‑high and no realistic draw now (the two clubs on board plus my A♣ make a flush impossible with one card to come). Claude is short (~870) and opened wide; his flop c‑bet was small and he can shove a very wide range. Betting out OOP with a marginal hand risks getting shoved or called by better hands (T‑x, pocket pairs, Qx) and unnecessarily bloats the pot. Checking controls pot size, keeps weaker parts of his range in, and preserves the option to fold to significant aggression. Plan: check — call a small/medium turn bet if it’s reasonable, but fold to a large shove (a shove of ~870 into the 1450 pot requires ~37.5% equity to call, which A‑high doesn’t have). If he checks behind I take the free showdown.

Betting 650 on the turn with AK against Minni's showing weakness. Minni checked twice (flop and turn), indicating a likely missed hand or draw. My premium AK is very probably best here. Value betting extracts chips while maintaining initiative and not giving free cards to potential draws. This sizing leaves me ~220 chips for river play if called.

Call 650. Pot is 2100 so I’m getting ~23.6% pot odds (650/(2100+650)). A♣5♥ has very limited raw improvement (only 3 A outs + 3 five outs ≈ 13% to pair on the river), but Claude is a short, wide-opening button who has been betting aggressively; his turn stab is a mid-sized pressure bet that will include a fair number of bluffs/missed broadways. I block a lot of Ax combinations with A♣ and checking/calling keeps the pot controllable and allows me to realize showdown equity without bloating it OOP. Raising here inflates the pot unnecessarily and will mostly force all‑ins or folds; folding here is too nitty versus a wide BTN range. Plan: call to see the river, fold to significant river aggression if I don’t improve, and otherwise evaluate river action (call small/medium bluff-sized bets or continue if I pair the A or 5).

Board: 2♣–6♣–T♠–Q♠–K♥. I have A♣5♥ — no pair, no realistic draw. Claude is very short (~220) and has been opening/pressuring wide, so his range contains both value (T, pairs, Qx, Kx) and bluffs. With the pot at 2,750 and me out of position, betting here as a bluff is unnecessary and likely to either get called by better hands or induce a shove I’m behind against; checking controls the pot and lets me see a showdown vs missed hands. If Claude shoves his remaining ~220 after I check, the pot odds are excellent (~220/(2750+220) ≈ 7.4%), so calling is justified versus a wide shove range. Plan: check and take showdown; call a small all‑in shove if it happens, but avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal Ace-high.

All-in river value bet with pair of Kings. Minni's river check indicates weakness after checking flop and turn. My As-Kc makes the best possible pair of Kings (K-K-A-Q-T) which beats his entire checking/calling range except premium pairs. With critical short stack of 220, I must maximize value on strong hands. Going all-in extracts value from his weaker holdings and gives proper odds for potential calls. This completes my aggressive 4-street value betting sequence with a premium hand.

Claude shoved his last ~220 into a pot showing 2,970 after I checked. This is effectively an all‑in for a very small amount relative to the pot. If I call 220 the total pot to win is ~3,190, so my break‑even equity is only ≈6.9% (220/3,190). Claude is very short and has been opening/playing a wide range; his river shove will contain both thin value (T, some pairs, K/Q combos) and a decent number of bluffs/missed broadways. I also hold A♣ which blocks a lot of Ax and club‑flush combos from his bluff/value mixes. Folding here would be overly nitty given the excellent pot odds; calling 220 is a low‑risk, +EV decision because I only need him to be bluffing a small fraction of the time to make the call profitable. I’ll call the all‑in to take the showdown.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in this position. My stack is significantly larger than my opponents', Minni (19.5 BB) and Claude (30.9 BB). I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and likely take down the blinds or play in position with a superior hand range.

I have Tc2c in the small blind facing a button open to 300. This is a very weak T2s hand — frequently dominated by the button's opening range and very awkward to play out of position against two opponents. To call 250 into a 450 pot requires ~36% equity just to break even, and with 19.5BB effective I don't want to invest a large chunk of my stack in a marginal, dominated spot. A 3‑bet bluff is unattractive here (I lack meaningful blockers and the pot commitment would be large if called or reraised). I fold and preserve my stack for better spots (position or stronger holdings).

2-10 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker with virtually no playability. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds to call (450 to 200), this is a clear fold situation. Flash has a large stack and has been aggressive throughout the session, suggesting a wide raising range from the button. With my relatively short stack of 3090, I need to be selective and preserve chips for premium hands. This weak hand has minimal equity against Flash's likely range, and even against a loose button raise, I'm dominated by most hands. Folding allows me to wait for a better spot to accumulate chips.

I’m on the button (19.5bb) with Q♦9♥ — a playable, but not premium, hand. Facing just the blinds (pot 150, to-call 100) I prefer to raise rather than limp or fold: position and fold equity make this a profitable steal attempt in a 3-handed game. I’ll size to 300 (3x BB) — it’s the table’s natural open size (seen frequently) and gives good fold equity without committing my stack. I don’t want to jam from 19.5bb with a marginal offsuit queen; if I face a large 3-bet (~900+ from the SB) I’ll be inclined to fold, while I can call smaller 3-bets with position and plan to use c-bets on favorable flops (Q/9 or dry boards) and control the pot on heavy, connected boards. Raise to 300 to seize initiative and play postflop in position.

8-3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker and should be folded from almost any position. In the small blind, facing a button raise of 300 from short-stacked Minni (1650 chips), with the big blind still to act, I have no reason to call. My hand has minimal equity, and even accounting for position and ICM dynamics, 8-3 is fundamentally unplayable. The fold is clear and correct.

I have AhTh in the big blind. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300, and Claude has folded. Minni's stack is very short (1650 total), while mine is substantial (34860). Given the strength of my hand and Minni's limited stack, this is a clear spot to either call or raise. However, since Minni is so short-stacked, putting them all-in or making a significant raise is a strong play to isolate. A 3-bet here puts immense pressure on a short stack. I will raise to 900, which commits more than half of Minni's stack and effectively signals that I am willing to play for her remaining chips.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♦9♥ and Flash (BB) 3-bet to 900. I’ve already invested 300 and would have to call 600 more with 1650 behind. The pot is 1250, so calling 600 into ~1850 requires roughly 32% equity to break even. Q9o offsuit is frequently dominated or behind vs a typical 3-betting range (pair+ broadways, suited aces, suited broadways) and doesn’t have the suit or playability to justify committing more chips. Reraising to the min (1500 total) would commit far too much with a marginal hand. With my effective stack, preserving chips for a better spot in position is the best play — fold here.

Calling with pocket deuces on the button. Low cost (100 chips) for favorable position, reasonable equity, and set-mining potential. The small blind is deep (35260) but I act last postflop. This is a standard profitable call.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 2c. Claude, the Button, has just limped for 100. The cost to complete is only 50 chips, and I have a suited hand that can occasionally flop well. Given the pot odds and the fact that I am out of position, completing the small blind is a reasonable low-cost play to see a flop. However, since my hand is very weak, folding is also an option, but in a three-handed game, seeing a flop cheaply with suited connectors/gappers is often acceptable.

I’m in the big blind with K♦ J♠ facing two limp callers (SB and BTN). KJ offsuit is playable but not strong enough to iso-raise multiway out of position — a raise to 200 will often be called and put me OOP against two players (one with a very deep stack), creating tricky postflop spots. I’ll check to see a flop for free and keep pot under control. Postflop plan: if I pair a K or J or pick up a strong draw I’ll fire for value/pressure; if I completely miss and face pressure from one or both opponents I’ll give up unless pot odds or equity justify a call.

On a flop of 3c-Ad-3h, the board is very dry and favors anyone holding an Ace or a 3. My hand, 4c 2c, has virtually no value other than a backdoor flush draw. In a three-way limped pot, there is no need to bloat the pot with a weak hand. I will check and see how the other players react to this board.

Flop is 3c Ad 3h; I hold Kd Js and completely missed. This is a multiway limp pot (SB checked), and with an Ace on board plus paired threes my hand has essentially no showdown value or usable draw and I don’t block enough strong Ax combos to credibly blast everyone off. Betting here as a bluff multiway risks getting called or raised by better hands; I want to keep the pot small and see how the Button reacts. Action: check to control the pot and evaluate — fold to any meaningful c-bet, only consider calling a very small probe if my read says it’s a bluff, otherwise proceed to the turn and reassess.

With pocket deuces on a flop of 3c-Ad-3h, my hand is currently very weak (just an unpaired pair of deuces). Both opponents have checked, indicating weak holdings on this ace-heavy, paired board. Given my medium-short stack (2940 chips) and the shallow effective stack depth with Minni (1550), I should preserve chips in this spot rather than bet into two opponents with minimal equity. Checking allows me to: (1) see the turn cheaply and potentially hit a set with another deuce, (2) avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand, and (3) maintain position to make better decisions postflop. The board texture (paired, ace-high) isn't favorable for a speculative bet with this holding. Discretion is warranted here.

The turn card is the Queen of Diamonds, making the board 3c-Ad-3h-Qd. I have 4-high and no real draws. After everyone checked the flop, there is still a possibility that someone is holding an Ace or just picked up a pair of Queens. There is no reason to bluff into two opponents with zero equity in a small pot. I will check and likely fold to any bet.

Board on the turn is 3c Ad 3h Qd; I hold Kd Js. I missed a pair but picked up a very strong diamond draw (Kd + Ad + Qd) — 10 outs to make a flush on the river (~21–22% equity). Both opponents checked the flop and Flash checked the turn, which looks like weakness in this multiway limp pot. A small-to-medium semi‑bluff is the correct line: it gives me fold equity to win the pot now, builds the pot when I hit, and keeps my commitment manageable against two deep opponents. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (150). If I get raised I will fold (I only have a draw and don’t want to commit my stack to a strong value hand). If called I’ll evaluate the river — go for value if I hit the flush, otherwise check/fold to significant pressure.

With pocket deuces on a board of 3c-Ad-3h-Qd, I have a severely diminished hand - just a pair of twos against a pair of threes on the board. My only realistic outs are the two remaining deuces to make a set (2 outs), giving me approximately 4% equity on the river. I'm being asked to call 150 chips into a 450-chip pot, which provides 3:1 odds but requires 25% equity to break even - far more than my ~4% actual equity. Minni's turn raise after checking the flop suggests strength or at minimum aggression that I cannot profitably contest with such a marginal holding. In a cash game focused on long-term profitability, this is a clear spot to preserve chips by folding. The board texture (paired, ace-high, queen showing) is particularly unfavorable for a speculative hand without sufficient pot odds. Discretion preserves my stack for more favorable opportunities.

I have 4-high on a board of 3c-Ad-3h-Qd. After a checked flop, Minni has led out for 150 into a 300-chip pot. I have zero showdown value and zero drawing equity. There is no reason to continue in this hand with such a weak holding, especially when I am out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with QTo, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. With the small blind having a very short stack (1,800 chips) and the big blind also relatively short (2,840 chips), I want to apply pressure and potentially pick up the blinds or see a flop in position. A standard open to 300 is effective here to force the short stacks to make a decision for a significant portion of their remaining chips.

8♣9♥ is a marginal, connected but offsuit hand. Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind I don’t want to 3‑bet with this hand (out of position and no suit equity). Folding is safe, but calling 250 more (300 total in the pot including my posted 50) is the right mix: it gets reasonable pot odds to see a flop, preserves most of my 1,800 stack, and keeps the button’s wide raising range in play. If Claude (BB) squeezes I’ll re-evaluate and most often fold to a sizable 3‑bet; postflop I’ll proceed cautiously — continue only with a pair/strong draw or use pot control when checked to. Action: call 300 total (add 250).

I'm holding 6h Ts (weak ten with poor kicker) in the big blind out of position against Flash's aggression and Minni's call. While the pot odds of 3.5:1 are reasonable, this hand is too weak to defend with two opponents already in the pot. I have 2840 chips and am becoming relatively short-stacked at this 50/100 level. Folding preserves chips for stronger spots where I can apply pressure or have better equity. My hand has minimal strength and being OOP against two players is a significant disadvantage that doesn't justify the call here.

I hold 9c8h and the flop is Kh 6d 9d — I have a pair of nines (middle/top depending on villain), out of position vs the button who opened preflop. This is a two-diamond board with a King high, so many of Flash’s value combos (Kx, sets) beat me and there are diamond draws to worry about. My hand has decent showdown value vs his wide button range but is vulnerable, so my priority is pot control and seeing a turn cheaply. Action: check. Plan: if Flash checks back we go to showdown cheap; if he C‑bets small (~≤1/3 pot ≈230) I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; if he sizes up large (>~1/2 pot) I’ll fold most of the time. I’ll only invest further if the turn improves my hand or the betting gives a clear read that I’m ahead.

On a King-high board of Kh-6d-9d, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and two overcards to the middle of the board. My opponent, Minni, is extremely short-stacked with only 1,550 chips remaining. While a continuation bet is standard for the pre-flop aggressor, Minni's short stack makes her more likely to play 'fit or fold' or even shove over a bet if she has any piece of the board or a draw. By checking back, I see a free turn card, preserve my stack against a potential desperate check-shove, and allow myself to realize the equity of my straight draw and overcards.

I have 9♣8♥ — a one‑pair (pair of nines) on K♥ 6♦ 9♦ with the A♠ on the turn. Pot is 700 and I’m out of position vs the button who checked the flop and has checked to me on the turn. Many of his value combos (A‑x, K‑x, sets) beat my pair and there are still diamond draws; betting here would inflate the pot and commit chips with a vulnerable, medium‑strength hand against a deep opponent. My objective is pot control and a cheap river showdown. I’ll check and see the river; if Flash fires a small bet I can call for pot odds and re‑evaluate, but I’ll fold to large aggression unless I improve. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid getting exploited.

The Ace on the turn is a scare card that generally favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. However, Minni is very short-stacked (1,550 chips), making her play unpredictable; she might check-shove with any pair or draw. Since I have a gutshot straight draw (Jack) and Queen-high, I have some showdown value if the river is friendly and some equity to improve. By checking behind again, I see a free river card, avoid the risk of being shoved off my equity by a desperate short stack, and keep the pot manageable. If I miss on the river, I can decide then whether to bluff or just give up based on her action.

I have one pair (9♣ with 8♥ kicker) on K♥ 6♦ 9♦ A♠ 4♦. Flash (button) has checked flop, turn and river — a very passive line that’s consistent with missed K/A, a missed diamond draw, or a medium/weak holding. The hands that beat me (Kx, Ax, two‑pair/sets, or a made two‑diamond flush) are plausible but many of those would have shown some aggression; betting here would unnecessarily inflate the pot and risk a large portion of my 1,550 stack for thin value. With pot = 700 and no bet to call, the prudent line is pot control and take this to showdown. Action: check; if Flash fires a small river bet I’ll typically call for pot odds, but I’ll fold to large river aggression unless I pick up a read showing him as bluffing.

The board has three diamonds and two overcards (Ace and King). I have checked the flop and turn, and Minni has checked all three streets. While my Queen-high is unlikely to win at showdown if she has any pair, Minni is playing a very short stack (1,550 chips). If I bet, I might induce a hero call from any pair or a diamond draw she was holding, and since I've checked twice, my hand doesn't look particularly strong. Given her stack size, she is likely to either fold air or call with any piece of the board. I will check behind and take my chances at showdown, as the risk of being check-raised or called by a short stack outweighs the likelihood of a successful bluff here.

I'm on the button with pocket 4s. Pot is 150 and I must call 100 to see a flop; my stack is 2250 (~22.5bb) and opponents are Claude (SB) 2790 and Flash (BB) 34810. With ~22bb set-mining is marginal — calling 100 to pursue a set is not a great long-term play. A small open (200–300) leaves me in a tough spot postflop and gives the big, deep-stacked player (Flash) good odds to see or shove over the top. Shoving maximizes fold equity from the SB and simplifies the decision-making: I either take down the pot immediately or go all-in with a pocket pair that has decent equity vs typical calling ranges. I prefer the shove here to pick up the blinds/isolate and avoid awkward postflop spots. Action: shove all-in (raise to my full stack).

2d4d is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While defending the blind is normally encouraged, the hand quality is too poor and my stack would be critically short (590 chips) after calling. Minni's raise from the button is strong enough that my 2-4o has minimal equity. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

Minni has shoved all-in from the button for 2250 chips. I am in the big blind with 8h 6h. To call, I need to commit 2150 more into a total pot of 4550, requiring approximately 47% equity. Against a standard button shoving range (pairs, Ax, Kx, and broadway cards), 8h 6h suited typically only has about 32-35% equity. Given my healthy stack of over 34,000 chips, there is no strategic reason to take a significantly -EV gamble here.

Calling 100 from the button with 7-6o is justified by excellent pot odds (2.5:1), positional advantage, and playability post-flop. With solid stack depth and two opponents already invested, I can realize my equity and exploit position with post-flop skill. Folding would be too tight here.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 5c. Claude, on the button, has limped in for 100. My hand is very weak and plays poorly out of position. While the price to call is only 50 chips more, 95 offsuit is not worth defending, especially with short stacks in the Big Blind and Button who are likely to shove or play aggressively post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have T♥2♦ in the big blind facing a limp from Claude (button) with the small blind folded. This is a very weak, off‑suit hand that plays poorly out of position postflop. There’s no incentive to invest more preflop to isolate — a raise would commit extra chips with little equity and open me to 3‑bet/pressure. I’ll check and see a cheap flop; plan is to fold to significant aggression unless I improve to a hand with showdown value or good redraws.

I’m on the big blind with T♥2♦ after checking preflop to a limp from Claude. Flop Qc‑9d‑7s gives me no pair and only backdoor, very low equity. Out of position and facing a limper’s wide range (Qx, 9x, 7x, medium pairs, broadways, spew). Best course is pot control: check and give up to aggression. I’ll check the flop and fold to any meaningful bet (most standard c‑bets here), only ever call an extremely tiny probe (much smaller than the usual 100 min) to see a cheap turn. If Claude checks back, I’ll reassess on the turn with a free card.

Top pair with weak kicker on a checked flop is a good spot to bet for value and control. Minni showed weakness with two checks (pre-flop and flop), making this a profitable value bet. Proper sizing maintains pot control while building value with a decent hand. Position and stack depth support aggressive play here.

I have T♥2♦ on Qc‑9d‑7s: no pair and no real draw (only extremely remote backdoor combos). Claude (button) has a wide but believable c‑bet range that includes Qx, 9x, 7x, overpairs and many hands that beat me. He bet 150 into a ~400 pot, so calling 150 requires ~27% equity (150/(400+150) ≈ 27%). My actual equity vs a standard c‑bet range is far below that, and calling OOP will put me in tough spots on later streets. Raising as a bluff is too risky with this holding and stack sizes. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine of clubs, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is significantly larger than both of my opponents, who have around 22-28 big blinds. Raising to 300 is a standard button open that allows me to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds. If called, J9s has good post-flop playability.

I’m in the small blind with 6♠8♣ facing a button open to 300 (3x). I’m shortish (~22.5bb) and out of position with a weak, unsuited connector that has poor postflop playability and realization OOP — especially with the big blind still to act. Calling 250 commits a meaningful portion of my stack into tough multiway/OOP spots; a small 3-bet to 500 won’t buy enough fold equity and could open me up to a shove or cold-call from Claude. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

7-5 offsuit is too weak to play from BB, especially out of position against Flash's aggressive button raise. With 28.4 BB stack, I should preserve chips for better situations. This is a clear fold - the hand has poor equity (~25-30%), plays terribly postflop, and a raise doesn't generate fold equity given my stack size.

3-handed on the button with Q2o. In a short-handed spot I should open a wide range to exploit position and steal the blinds; Q2o is marginal but fine to open here. I’ll raise to 300 (standard 3x the BB) to generate fold equity and avoid giving the BB good pot odds to see a cheap flop. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this weak queen; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or exercise pot control and fold to heavy resistance.

K6o is a weak hand to play from the small blind, particularly after Minni's raise and with Flash still in the big blind. My stack is 2790 chips, which is relatively short, so I need to be selective about where I commit chips. This hand doesn't warrant playing here - it's dominated by most of Minni's opening range, and I have no flush or straight draws. I should preserve chips for stronger spots like premium pairs or broadway cards where I have better equity. This is a standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9d 5c. The player on the Button (Minni) has raised to 300. Although it only costs me 200 more to call into a pot of 450, 95o is an extremely weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a short stack where my potential for strategic maneuvering is limited. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I'm holding K♦ K♥, one of the strongest starting hands in poker, on the button with good position. While my stack (2790) is shorter than Flash's (34760), I need to play premium hands aggressively to maximize value and take down pots. A standard 3x big blind raise to 300 is appropriate here: it's a strong, standard sizing that builds the pot, extracts value, and maintains proper aggression with premium pocket pairs. This raise also gives me better information about my opponents' holdings and positions me well to continue playing the hand profitably postflop. The pot odds remain good for potential callers, but the raise establishes my strength immediately.

I am in the Small Blind holding 5c 6h. Claude (Button) has raised to 300. Both opponents have relatively short stacks (around 23-25 BBs). 56o is a very weak hand and playing it out of position against a Button raise is generally a losing strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a button open to 300 from the big blind with Kc Jd and stacks ~23–25bb. Calling 200 (300 total committed) gives me ~3.25:1 pot odds and good equity against a wide BTN opening range heads-up. A min 3-bet to 500 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and a shove would be overcommitting with a marginal but playable hand. I'll call to see a flop and proceed: continue aggressively if I pair a K/J or pick up strong draws, fold to overwhelming aggression on dry boards.

Out of position vs the preflop raiser on a very dry Q♣ 7♠ 2♦ flop. I have K♣ J♦ — only one overcard (K) and essentially no draw (no realistic flush/straight with two cards to come). Claude will c‑bet this board a lot, so checking is the prudent default: I control the pot size, keep his bluffs in, and preserve my stack (~21bb) against larger pressure. Plan: check and call a standard c‑bet (small→medium, roughly up to ~2/3 pot ≈ 400–430 chips) to realize equity and look to improve; fold to significant raises or very large barrels on turn if I don’t improve. If I pair K or J on a later street I can value bet/raise; otherwise avoid bloating the pot OOP with just an overcard.

Betting 450 on the flop with pocket kings on a dry 7-2-Q board. Minni checked to me, indicating a weak hand or a hand in need of help. This bet size extracts value from weaker holdings, builds the pot with my premium pair, and charges draws. With short stacks on both sides (2490 and 2100), I'm committing to the hand appropriately while maintaining enough chips for postflop decision-making. Standard value bet continuation.

Out of position vs the preflop opener on a very dry Q♣ 7♠ 2♦ flop. I hold K♣ J♦ — one overcard and a backdoor club but no made hand or real draw. Claude’s bet to 450 into a 1,100 pot (~41% pot) is a touch larger than my earlier comfort threshold but calling 450 gives me ~29% break-even equity (450 to win 1,550). Versus a standard button opening/c-bet range (lots of missed broadways, some Qx, small pairs), my hand has roughly ~30–35% showdown equity, so a call to realize equity is justified. Raising OOP to the min (900) would be too committal and a shove target; folding here would be conceding too much given the pot odds and his wide c-bet frequency. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and only get aggressive if I pair a K/J or complete the club draw.

Turn: board 7s 2d Qc 2c, pot 1,550, my stack 1,650. I hold Kc Jd — a single overcard (K) and essentially no realistic draws (flush is impossible now; only three clubs total including my Kc, only one card left). Claude was the BTN preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop; that line includes lots of Qx, small pairs and some bluffs. Betting OOP here is thin: I have poor value (K‑high rarely gets called by worse) and limited fold equity against a BTN opening/c‑betting range, while a raise would put me in a very uncomfortable spot. Checking controls the pot, lets me see the river cheaply, and preserves stack to fold to genuine strength. Plan: check to the river; fold to a meaningful bet on the river unless I pair up or the river card and betting pattern give a clear bluffing spot.

Betting 600 on the turn with pocket kings on a dry 7-2-Q-2 board. Minni has checked twice, indicating a weak hand. This bet extracts value from inferior holdings (underpairs, weak queens, missed draws) while building the pot. With Minni's remaining stack at ~1650, this bet puts meaningful pressure on their decision-making without being so large as to be exploitable. The turn brings a second deuce, which isn't concerning—I'm still ahead of Minni's likely range. Standard value bet sizing that maximizes chips won while keeping him engaged.

Fold. The turn bet requires ~600/(2150+600)=21.8% equity to call, but KcJd only has ~6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 Js) → ~13% to pair on the river and very little additional showdown equity. There is no realistic draw (flush impossible with one card to come) and Claude’s line (PF open -> flop c‑bet -> turn barrel after I checked) is much more consistent with Qx, a set/boat, or other value hands than with frequent bluffs. A raise would be a very thin bluff and overcommits a short stack vs a player who’s shown aggressive value. Preserve chips and fold to the turn bet.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives. Both opponents have very short stacks relative to the blinds and my own stack (Minni has 1600, Claude has 3490). A standard open-raise is effective here to put pressure on them and potentially take the blinds, or to set up a situation where I can comfortably call a shove if they decide to jam, given the strength of pocket fives in short-handed play against short stacks.

I’m in the SB with A♣7♥ facing a button open to 300 and I have 1,600 (16BB).
